title: The Difference Between a Trigger and a Preference
researcherTopic: somatic experiencing, completing the stress response
metaDescription: Learn the crucial difference between a nervous system trigger and a simple preference. Understanding this distinction is key to healing complex trauma.
imageDescription: Golden light illuminates the delicate, intricate web of a neural network. The light flows through the pathways, suggesting healing and the gentle repair of a complex system. The overall feeling is one of warmth, hope, and the beauty of resilience.
htmlContent:
Peter Levine’s work in somatic experiencing illuminates a fundamental distinction that many of us living with the echoes of trauma struggle to parse - the difference between a genuine nervous system trigger and a simple, uncomplicated preference. We might say we’re “triggered” by loud noises, by a certain tone of voice, or by the feeling of being rushed, when in fact, we simply prefer quiet, calm, and a slower pace. The distinction is not semantic; it is a critical fork in the road of recovery, one path leading toward deeper self-awareness and the other toward a life inadvertently organized around avoidance. It is the beginning of a more honest conversation with our own physiology, a conversation that moves beyond the simplistic language of likes and dislikes into the far more detailed territory of safety and threat, a terrain our bodies know intimately even if our conscious minds have long since lost the map. This is the subtle, crucial work of learning to listen to the body’s native tongue, a language spoken not in words, but in sensation, impulse, and the deep, quiet knowing that precedes all thought.

The Architecture of Preference

A preference is an expression of the self, a gentle inclination toward one thing over another that arises from a place of relative stability and choice. It is the simple joy of a particular kind of tea, the comfort of a worn sweater, the quiet satisfaction of an organized desk - these are the textures of a life lived with a degree of agency. Preferences are born in the prefrontal cortex, the part of our brain responsible for personality expression, complex decision-making, and executive function. They are flexible, context-dependent, and can evolve over time as we change and grow, reflecting our learning and our values. In my years of working in this territory, I’ve seen how reclaiming one’s preferences can be a quiet act of rebellion against the homogenizing effect of trauma, which often demands we shrink ourselves, our desires, and our expressions to a manageable, predictable, and often invisible state to survive. When we can say, “I like this,” or "I prefer that," without needing to justify it or defend it, we are standing on the ground of our own being. A preference does not carry the electrical charge of a survival response; it is a whisper, not a scream. It is the difference between enjoying solitude and being unable to tolerate the presence of others for fear of what might happen. Learning to honor our preferences is a vital part of re-inhabiting a body that may have felt like a foreign country for a very long time, a slow and steady process of repatriation into the homeland of the self. It is a quiet revolution of reclaiming the small joys that trauma so often steals, a way of repopulating our inner world with color and texture after a long winter of gray. It is the practice of remembering that we are not just a collection of symptoms and survival responses, but a person with a unique and valid way of being in the world.

The Signature of a Trigger

A trigger, by contrast, is not a choice or a reflection of personality; it is a physiological alarm bell, a non-negotiable, whole-system response to a perceived threat that the body remembers even if the conscious mind does not. It is an echo of the past erupting into the present moment, bypassing the rational brain entirely and hijacking the nervous system into a state of high alert. What emerges is not a matter of not liking something; it is a matter of the body screaming “DANGER!” with every fiber of its being, mobilizing for a threat that is no longer present. Triggers are the domain of the amygdala and the brainstem, the ancient, reptilian parts of our neurobiology dedicated to one thing: survival. When a trigger is activated, the carefully constructed edifice of the present moment collapses, and we are plunged back into the timeless, terrifying field of the original trauma. This is the legacy of what Peter Levine calls the incomplete stress response, a survival instinct that was thwarted and is now trapped in the body, endlessly seeking resolution. It is a striking and often bewildering experience, one that can leave us feeling broken and out of control. Understanding this distinction is crucial, as it allows us to stop blaming ourselves for what is, in essence, a brilliant but outdated survival strategy. We can begin to see these responses not as a pathology, but as a testament to what we have endured and the incredible intelligence of a system that did whatever it took to get us through. It is the body’s loyalty to a past that the mind is trying to forget.

Mapping the Internal World

The High Cost of a Triggered Life

From Reaction to Relationship

The path from a life of reaction to a life of relationship... with ourselves, with others, and with the world - is the essence of healing from complex trauma. It is the slow, painstaking work of building enough safety in the present moment to be able to tolerate the echoes of the past without being consumed by them. This involves cultivating what the contemplative traditions call “witness consciousness,” the ability to observe our experience without being identified with it. We learn to see the trigger arise, to feel the cascade of sensations and emotions it unleashes, and to hold it all in a larger container of awareness. We say, “Ah, this is a trigger. What we find here is the memory of danger, not the presence of it.” In that moment, a space opens up. It is the space between stimulus and response, and in that space lies our power. We can choose to breathe. We can choose to remind ourselves that we are here, now, and that we are safe. This is not a one-time event; it is a practice, a moment-by-moment recommitment to the present. It is the difference between being thrown about by the waves and learning to surf them. Over time, as we build this internal muscle, the triggers begin to lose their power. They may still arise, but they no longer define our reality. They become what they are: energetic signatures of the past, visitors that come and go, but no longer have the authority to evict us from our own home. We can even begin to find the wisdom in the wound, recognizing that these triggers are pointers to the exact places within us that are calling for attention and healing. They become our teachers, not our tormentors, guiding us back to the parts of ourselves we have long abandoned.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link).

For further reading and professional resources, visit Psychology Today's therapist directory.

The Sovereignty of Choice

Ultimately, the distinction between a trigger and a preference is a gateway to a more sovereign life. It is the difference between a life dictated by the ghosts of the past and a life chosen in the clarity of the present. To live a sovereign life is to have the capacity to choose our responses, to act from a place of wisdom rather than from a place of compulsion. It is to know that while we may not be able to control what happens to us, we can cultivate a relationship with our own experience that allows us to move through the world with a sense of agency and grace. What emerges is not about being invulnerable or “fixed.” It is about being deeply, resiliently human. It is about understanding that our nervous system is not our enemy, but a faithful servant doing its best to keep us safe based on old information. Our work is to gently, patiently update that information, to show our bodies, through lived experience, that the war is over. This is how we reclaim the territory of our own lives, one conscious choice at a time. It is how we learn that the past does not have to be a life sentence. It can be a reference library, one that we can learn to consult without having to live in it. This is the heart of neuroception, our nervous system's ability to distinguish between safety and danger, and our conscious ability to influence it. This is the foundation of a life where we are not just surviving, but thriving, a life where the past informs but no longer defines us. It is the beginning of a life lived from the inside out.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link).

But what would it take to truly live that distinction? What if, for one week, you treated every strong reaction not as an indictment of the world, but as a simple request for information from your own body? What might you learn if your only goal was not to fix or to solve, but simply to listen to the answer?

Frequently Asked Questions

How can I tell if I'm just being "too sensitive"?

The concept of being "too sensitive" is often a form of self-invalidation rooted in external criticism. The real question is not about the volume of your sensitivity, but its source. The work is to honor the body's signals as valid information rather than judging them as an inconvenience or a character flaw.

Can a preference become a trigger?

While they are distinct, the line can become blurred. If a simple preference ~ for instance, for a quiet environment ~ is repeatedly violated in a way that feels disempowering or unsafe, the nervous system can begin to associate that preference with a threat. It turns out to be why understanding and honoring our own capacity, and communicating our needs before we reach a breaking point, is so important.

For managing sensory overwhelm, a tool that often helps is Sony WH-1000XM5 Noise Canceling Headphones (paid link).

Stay with me here.

If I avoid my triggers, won't they just go away?

Avoidance is a short-term strategy that often creates long-term problems. While it's wise to manage your exposure to overwhelming stimuli, consistent avoidance reinforces the nervous system's belief that the trigger is, in fact, a catastrophic threat. The healing path involves gradually and intentionally building the capacity to be with the discomfort of a trigger in a safe, contained way, which allows the trapped survival energy to resolve itself and the nervous system to learn that it is safe now.

A fidget tool that keeps hands busy during anxious moments is Speks Magnetic Balls (paid link).

The hard part was never the knowing. It was always the doing.
